title: The S-Curve (Sigmoid)
concept: The Sigmoid function squashes any real number into a probability between 0 and 1, creating an S-shaped curve.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary difference between regression and classification?

options.text: Regression predicts continuous numbers, while classification predicts discrete categories.
Regression only uses linear equations, while classification never uses equations.
Regression is used for small datasets, while classification is only for big data.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Regression estimates continuous values (like prices), while classification assigns data to distinct categories.

cards.title: The breaking point
cards.body: Imagine using a straight line to predict if study hours lead to passing a test (yes=1, no=0). At 20 hours of study, your line might predict a 150% probability of passing! But probabilities can never exceed 100%.

cards.title: The Sigmoid function
cards.body: To fix this, we use the Sigmoid function. It is a mathematical curve that takes any real number and squashes it into a value strictly between 0 and 1.

cards.title: The dimmer switch
cards.body: Think of a dimmer switch with hard stops at off (0) and full brightness (1). No matter how hard you turn the dial, you can never go below pitch black or above maximum light.

cards.title: Squashing values
cards.body: If a linear formula outputs a raw score of 5, the Sigmoid function squashes it to 0.99 (99% probability). A raw score of 0 squashes to exactly 0.50 (50% probability).

cards.title: Soft choices, not hard cuts
cards.body: A common myth is that the Sigmoid function instantly categorizes data into 0 or 1. Instead, it outputs a decimal probability (like 0.73). We only convert that to a hard category later by applying a threshold.

cards.title: Linear vs. Sigmoid Output
cards.body: Notice how the output behaves for extreme inputs:

left.text:
Can output any number.
Goes to infinity.
Outputs like -0.5 or 1.3 make no sense for probabilities.

right.text:
Bounded between 0 and 1.
Bends at the extremes.
Perfect for modeling probabilities.

exercises.prompt: What is the mathematical range of outputs produced by the Sigmoid function?

options.text: Any real number from negative to positive infinity

exercises.explain: Perfect! The Sigmoid function always outputs a value between 0 and 1, matching the required range of probabilities.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An input of exactly 0 to the Sigmoid function results in an output of 0.5.
exercises.explain: True! A score of 0 represents the center of the Sigmoid curve, translating to a probability of 0.5 (50% chance).

exercises.sentence: To convert any raw linear model score into a probability, we pass it through the ___ function.
exercises.explain: Great job! The Sigmoid function (also called the logistic function) squashes any input into a probability.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following outputs could be produced directly by a Sigmoid function?
exercises.explain: Exactly! Sigmoid outputs must lie strictly between 0 and 1. Values like 1.25 or -0.15 are mathematically impossible.

exercises.prompt: You are building a medical AI to predict if a patient will be readmitted to the hospital. Your linear equation output for a patient is a very high positive number (e.g., +10). When passed through the Sigmoid function, what probability of readmission does the model predict?
exercises.explain: Spectacular! Very high positive inputs get squashed to nearly 1.0, indicating high confidence. Congratulations on mastering the Sigmoid function! You now know how S-curves keep probabilities in check!
